title: Soil Organic Carbon map of Cote d’Ivoire
text:
Introduction
t4_soc_map is part of the terri4sol project.
All t4_soc_map analyses rely on the quarto documents (files.qmd) that can be run with R and associated environment defined with renv.

title: Soil Organic Carbon map of Cote d’Ivoire
text:
Project
t4_soc_map includes:

Analyse of the data with associated documentation and figures:

Reproducible analyses in files.qmd
Resulting pages in docs/
Document structure definition in _quarto.yml

Data in data/
R scripts with functions in r/
Intermediary files in outputs/
Figures in figures/
R environment definition with renv in renv/ and renv/lock
Git and GitHub files (.gitignore , .github/)
Project documentation (README.qmd , README.md , LICENSE)

title: Soil Organic Carbon map of Cote d’Ivoire
text:
Help
Please preferentially create an issue on GitHub for any questions, bugs or help needed regarding t4_soc_map: https://github.com/cpiponiot/t4s_soc_map/issues. However, you can contact us by email through people in the core group (see below).

title: Open and prepare the raw dataset
text:
A summary of the data from the terri4sol project is presented below.

data_t4s <- read.csv("data/soil_1.1.0.csv")

Soil organic carbon stocks were estimated as
\[ stock = C \cdot (\Delta d \cdot area) \cdot fine \cdot bd\]
where \(C\) is the soil organic carbon content, \(\Delta d\) is the measurement depth (0.1 m), \(area\) is the standardisation area (1 ha = \(10^4\) m), \(fine\) is the fine fraction of the soil, and \(bd\) is the bulk density of the fine fraction (in Mg m\(^3\)).


Code
data_t4s <- data_t4s |>
  mutate(type = ifelse(is.na(C), "spectro", "chim")) |>
  mutate(C = ifelse(is.na(C), C_spectro, C)) |>
  mutate(stock = C / 100 * 1e3 * fine_fraction * bulk_density_g_cm3)


We also added data from the RévaTéné project that collected SOC data in the Téné forest plots.


Code
data_tene <- read_excel("data/BdD_Mathus_TENE.xls", 2) |>
  mutate(T_stock = `CCOS_0-30cm` / 100 *
           (`Dapp_0-10cm` + `Dapp_10-20cm` + `Dapp_20-30cm`) / 3 *
           (1 - `%EG_0-30cm`) * 1e3) |>
  mutate(site = "tene", plot = SUBPLOT) |>
  separate(SUBPLOT, c("pl", "sub")) |>
  reframe(get_tene_coords(pl, sub), across()) |>
  select(site, plot, lon, lat, T_stock, corg = `CCOS_0-30cm`)


We grouped the following sites because of their spatial overlap: “tene” and “tene_oume”; “adzope”, “adzopeN”, “yaya” and “mebifon”.


Code
read.csv("data/plot_1.1.0.csv") |>
  filter(lat > 0 & !(lat > 6.2 & site == "yaya")) |>
  filter(grepl("adzope|mebifon|yaya|tene", site)) |>
  mutate(new_site = ifelse(grepl("tene", site), "tene", "adzope")) |>
  ggplot(aes(lon, lat, col = site)) +
  geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(~new_site, scales = "free") +
  theme_bw()









We then sum the carbon stocks over all depths at each site to get the total carbon stocks, and save the results, together with SOC content.


Code
read.csv("data/plot_1.1.0.csv") |>
  # correct plot coordinates
  mutate(lonc = ifelse(lon > 0, lat, lon), latc = ifelse(lat < 0, lon, lat)) |>
  select(site, plot, lonc, latc) |>
  rename(lon = lonc, lat = latc) |>
  inner_join(data_t4s) |>
  group_by(site, plot, lon, lat) |>
  summarise(T_stock = sum(stock), corg = mean(C), .groups = "drop", 
            type = paste(unique(type), collapse = "+")) |>
  # add revatene data
  bind_rows(data_tene) |>
  # group sites
  mutate(plot = paste(site, plot, sep = "_")) |>
  mutate(site = ifelse(grepl("tene", site), "tene", site)) |>
  mutate(site = ifelse(grepl("adzope|mebifon|yaya", site), "adzope", site)) |>
  mutate(site = ifelse(grepl("adk", site), "adk", site)) |>
  write.csv("data/cache/data_stocks.csv", row.names = FALSE)

title: Ancillary data
text:
Code
library(stringr)
library(googledrive)


We compiled a suite of topographic, spectral, thermal, and climatic predictors from publicly available remote-sensing products and climate datasets. All variables were processed to a common spatial resolution of 100 m to ensure consistency among layers.

title: Climate data
Spectral variables
Thermal variable
text:
Code
library(terra)
library(gdalUtilities)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggcorrplot)
library(ggfortify)


Land surface temperature (LST) was obtained from the MODIS MOD11A1 v6.1 dataset (1 km native resolution). Low-quality observations were removed using MODIS quality flags, and a 2024 annual mean was calculated. The resulting composite was spatially upscaled to 100 m.


Code
lst <-
  "data/01_Data/Environmental_variables/LST_Annual_Mean_2024_100m_CI.tif" |>
  rast()
plot(lst)











Code
# extract values for all t4s plots
coord_stocks <- read.csv("data/cache/data_stocks.csv") |>
  # project into EPSG:32630
  vect(crs = "epsg:4326") |>
  terra::project("epsg:32630")
thermal_data <- lst |>
  terra::extract(coord_stocks) |>
  select(-ID) |>
  bind_cols(select(as.data.frame(coord_stocks), "site", "plot")) |>
  relocate(site, plot)
write.csv(thermal_data, file = "data/cache/thermal_data.csv", row.names = FALSE)


The following plots have NAs in at least one Bioclim variable.


Code
thermal_data |>
  filter(if_any(everything(), is.na))

title: Topography
text:
Distribution of values in the t4s dataset
Elevation, slope, and aspect were derived from the Shuttle Radar Topography Mission (SRTM v3) digital elevation model (USGS SRTMGL1_003; 30 m resolution). Slope and aspect were computed from the original elevation data in Google Earth Engine, after which all bands were resampled to 100 m.
Topographical Wetness Index (TWI) was calculated using the same SRTM source. First, the 30 m DEM was aggregated to 100 m using a mean reducer. Sinks in the resampled DEM were filled using the Fill Sinks (Wang and Liu 2006) algorithm implemented in the SAGA GIS library (minimum slope = 0°). The SAGA Wetness Index module was then used to compute TWI (parameters: Suction = 10; Area = Specific Catchment Area; Slope type = Catchment slope; Minimum slope = 0; Offset slope = 0.1; Slope weighting = 1).
Code
read.csv("data/cache/topo_data.csv") |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c("elevation", "slope", "aspect", "twi")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = value)) +
  geom_density() +
  facet_wrap(~name, scales = "free") +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(x = NULL)
Code
read.csv("data/cache/topo_data.csv") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = elevation, slope, col = site)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_x_log10() +
  scale_y_log10() +
  theme_classic()
Code
read.csv("data/cache/topo_data.csv") |>
  left_join(read.csv("data/plot_0.2.6.csv")) |>
  filter(!is.na(toposequence) & toposequence != "") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = toposequence, y = slope)) +
  geom_violin(fill = "grey") +
  theme_classic()

title: Topography
text: Wang, Lei, and Hongxing Liu. 2006. “An Efficient Method for Identifying and Filling Surface Depressions in Digital Elevation Models for Hydrologic Analysis and Modelling.” International Journal of Geographical Information Science 20 (2): 193–213.

title: Landuse data
text:
Comparison with landuse recorded in the t4s dataset
We used the 2020 land use map of Côte d’Ivoire produced by the Bureau National d’Études Techniques et de Développement (BNEDT).
In the table below we compare the landuse values recorded in the terri4sol database (columns) with the landuse values extracted from the BNEDT map at the same locations (rows).
Code
read.csv("data/plot_1.1.0.csv") |> 
  mutate(plot = paste(site, plot, sep = "_")) |>
  mutate(site = ifelse(grepl("tene", site), "tene", site)) |>
  mutate(site = ifelse(grepl("adzope|mebifon|yaya", site), "adzope", site)) |>
  mutate(site = ifelse(grepl("adk", site), "adk", site)) |>
  right_join(read.csv("data/cache/data_stocks.csv")) |>
  select(site, plot, present_landuse) |>
  mutate(present_landuse = ifelse(site == "tene" & is.na(present_landuse),
    "forest", present_landuse
  )) |>
  mutate(present_landuse = ifelse(grepl("for", present_landuse),
    "forest", present_landuse
  )) |>
  write.csv("data/cache/data_landuse.csv", row.names = FALSE)
Code
read.csv("data/cache/bnedt_data.csv") |>
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/data_landuse.csv")) |>
  select(landuse, present_landuse) |>

Code
error_rate <- read.csv("data/cache/bnedt_data.csv") |>
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/data_landuse.csv")) |>
  mutate(valid = ifelse(
    present_landuse == "cassava" & grepl("Agricultural development", landuse) |
      present_landuse == "cocoa" & grepl("Cocoa", landuse) |
      present_landuse == "fallow" & grepl("Fallow|Secondary|Light", landuse) |
      present_landuse == "forest" & grepl("orest", landuse) |
      present_landuse == "food_crop" & grepl("Agricultural", landuse) |
      present_landuse == "palm" & grepl("palm", landuse) |
      present_landuse == "rubber" & grepl("Rubber", landuse) |
      present_landuse == "trees plantation" & grepl("plantation", landuse),
    TRUE, FALSE
  )) |>
  filter(!present_landuse %in% c("jvc", "old_mining", "others"))


Joining with `by = join_by(site, plot)`


Code
error_rate |>
  group_by(present_landuse) |>
  summarise(error_rate = sum(!valid) / length(valid))

title: TMF data
text:
Comparison of TMF and field-based plot classification
We used data from the Tropical Moist Forests (TMF) product, which is based on Landsat images (Vancutsem et al. 2021), to estimate the time since deforestation and the age of secondary forests. These classify tropical areas annually as follows: (i) undisturbed forests, (ii) disturbed forests, (iii) deforested areas, (iv) regrowing forests, (v) water, and (vi) other.
We estimated the age of secondary forests in 2022 as the number of years a pixel of regrowing forest in 2022 had continuously been classified as such. We estimated the time since deforestation as the time since a pixel that was classified as undisturbed or disturbed forest in 1990 (i.e. the first year of the TMF time series) had changed to another class.
We smoothed the time since deforestation (setting time = 0 for remaining forests) by averaging it with a 2D Gaussian kernel moving window. The standard deviation of the Guassian kernel was set to 300 m.
For plots that were recorded as forest or fallow, we used a deforestation age of 0. Plots in areas already deforested in 1990 were attributed a deforestation age of 40.
Code
tmf2022 <- rast("data/download/TMF/JRC_TMF_AnnualChange_v1_2022_RCI.tif") |>
  terra::extract(read.csv("data/cache/data_stocks.csv")[, c("lon", "lat")]) |>
  mutate(tmf_class = c(
    "Undisturbed", "Degraded", "Deforested", "Regrowth", "Water", "Other"
  )[Dec2022]) |>
  bind_cols(read.csv("data/cache/data_stocks.csv")) |>
  select(site, plot, tmf_class)

title: TMF data
text: Vancutsem, Christelle, Frédéric Achard, J-F Pekel, Ghislain Vieilledent, Silvia Carboni, Dario Simonetti, Javier Gallego, Luiz EOC Aragao, and Robert Nasi. 2021. “Long-Term (1990–2019) Monitoring of Forest Cover Changes in the Humid Tropics.” Science Advances 7 (10): eabe1603.

title: Soil types
Predictive Moran’s Eigenvector Maps
text:
Code
library(terra)
library(tidyverse)
library(pMEM)
source("r/qrf_llo.R")


These maps describe the spatial structure in the data, and were added as predictors in the Random Forest Model. They are based on the methodology described in Guénard and Legendre (2024).


Code
data <- read.csv("data/cache/data_stocks.csv") %>%
  drop_na()
coords <- data %>%
  vect(geom = c("lon", "lat"), crs = "EPSG:4326") %>%
  project("EPSG:32630") %>%
  crds()
data <- bind_cols(coords, data)

# final grid (100 m resolution)
new_coords <-
  "data/01_Data/SRTM.tif" %>%
  rast() %>%
  crds()

# define distance metric + weighting (improve by optimizing)
sites <- unique(data$site)
train_sites <- sample(sites, size = floor(0.8 * length(sites)))
train <- which(data$site %in% train_sites)
test <- which(!data$site %in% train_sites)

dwf_choice <- estimate_sef(
  x = as.matrix(data[train, c("x", "y")]),
  xx = as.matrix(data[test, c("x", "y")]),
  y = data$corg[train],
  yy = data$corg[test],
  lower = c(1e3),
  upper = c(1e5),
  list_fun = c("exponential", "Gaussian")
)
sel <- getMinMSE(
  y = data$corg[train],
  yy = data$corg[test],
  U = as.matrix(dwf_choice$sef),
  Up = predict(dwf_choice$sef, coords[test, ])
)

data_sites <- data %>%
  group_by(site) %>%
  summarise(x = mean(x), y = mean(y)) %>%
  mutate(train = site %in% train_sites)

new_coords_vis <- expand.grid(
  x = seq(-30000, 503000, 1e4),
  y = seq(520000, 1090000, 1e4)
)
predict(dwf_choice$sef, new_coords_vis, wh = sel$ord[1:sel$wh]) %>%
  bind_cols(data.frame(new_coords)) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = contains("pMEM")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x, y)) +
  geom_raster(aes(fill = value)) +
  geom_point(data = data_sites, aes(col = train)) +
  facet_wrap(~name) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c()

# predictions
if (!dir.exists("data/cache/pmem")) dir.create("data/cache/pmem")
index <- split(seq_len(nrow(new_coords)), cut(seq_len(nrow(new_coords)), 20))
for (i in index) {
  predict(dwf_choice$sef, new_coords[i, ], wh = sel$ord[1:sel$wh]) %>%
    bind_cols(new_coords[i, ]) %>%
    write.csv(paste0("data/cache/pmem/tile_", i[1], ".csv"), row.names = FALSE)
}

# join results
list.files("data/cache/pmem/", pattern = "tile", full.names = TRUE) |>
  lapply(fread) |>
  rbindlist(use.names = TRUE) |>
  select(x, y, contains("pMEM")) |>
  rast(crs = "EPSG:32630", type = "xyz") |>
  writeRaster("data/cache/pMEM.tif", overwrite = TRUE)




Code
# extract values for all t4s plots
coord_stocks <- read.csv("data/cache/data_stocks.csv") |>
  # project into EPSG:32630
  vect(crs = "epsg:4326") |>
  terra::project("epsg:32630")
pmem_data <-
  "data/cache/pMEM.tif" |>
  rast() |>
  terra::extract(coord_stocks) |>
  select(contains("pMEM")) |>
  bind_cols(select(as.data.frame(coord_stocks), "site", "plot")) |>
  relocate(site, plot)
write.csv(pmem_data, file = "data/cache/pmem_data.csv", row.names = FALSE)




Code
"data/cache/pMEM.tif" |>
  rast() |>
  plot()













Guénard, Guillaume, and Pierre Legendre. 2024. “Spatially explicit predictions using spatial eigenvector maps” 2024 (March): 2129–40. https://doi.org/10.1111/2041-210X.14413.

title: Methodological choices
text:
We tested different methodological choices: (i) adding a variable selection step to reduce the number of predictors; (ii) adding a spatially-explicit component.
We evaluated model performance using a leave-one-site-out cross-validation approach. The dataset was partitioned by sampling sites, such that all observations from a given site were withheld as the test set, while the remaining sites were used for model training. For each iteration, model predictions were compared to observed values in the test fold. Model accuracy was quantified using the root mean squared error (RMSE) and the coefficient of determination (R²), averaged across all validation folds.
To quantify prediction uncertainty while accounting for the spatial aggregation of the calibration data, we combined a leave-one-site-out (LOSO) cross-validation strategy with quantile regression. Specifically, we iteratively excluded one calibration site at a time and fitted a random forest model to the remaining data, yielding a set of s models (s corresponding to the number of sites). For each model, we generated new predictions by drawing 100 samples from the distribution of its predicted values. The resulting s predictive distributions were then pooled into a single aggregated distribution. From this combined distribution, we extracted the 10th, 50th (median), and 90th percentiles to represent the lower, central, and upper bounds of the prediction uncertainty, respectively.

title: Variable selection
text:
RandomForest without variable selection
A Random Forest (RF) model was implemented using the ranger package in R to predict soil organic carbon stock from environmental and soil covariates.
Code
formula_soc_all <- colnames(data_all) %>%
  setdiff(c("site", "plot", "lon", "lat", "T_stock", "corg")) %>%
  paste(collapse = " + ") %>%
  paste("corg ~", .) %>%
  as.formula()
training_index <- lapply(
  unique(data_all$site),
  function(s) which(data_all$site != s)
)
rf_all <- train(
  form = formula_soc_all,
  data = data_all,
  method = "rf",
  metric = "RMSE",
  trControl = trainControl(method = "cv", index = training_index),
  ntree = 500, importance = TRUE
)
save(rf_all, file = "data/cache/models/rf_all.rda")
Model performance was evaluated with a leave-one-site-out cross-validation, which yielded the following metrics:
RMSE: 0.4603164 
R²: 0.1629922

title: Variable selection
section: RandomForest with variable selection based on VIF
text:
RandomForest with variable selection based on VIF
Variable selection was done to avoid collinearity, by applying a using a Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) threshold of < 5 with a stepwise procedure on all variables (Naimi et al. 2014).


Code
# create groups
data_groups_vif <- data.frame(
  variable = setdiff(
    colnames(data_all),
    c("site", "plot", "lon", "lat", "T_stock", "corg")
  )
) |> # vif selection by group, based on variables selected at previous stage
  mutate(vif = vif_select(variable, data_all))
list_vars <- data_groups_vif |>
  filter(vif) |>
  select(variable) |>
  unlist() |>
  paste(collapse = "; ")


The variables kept were: bio01; bio06; bio12; Blue_mean; RedEdge4_mean; SWIR1_mean; slope; aspect; twi; Annual_Mean_LST; present_landuse; def_age; WRB2; pMEM_2; pMEM_1; pMEM_18; pMEM_10; pMEM_247; pMEM_29; pMEM_119; pMEM_528; pMEM_140; pMEM_219.


Code
formula_soc_vif <- data_groups_vif %>%
  filter(vif) %>%
  select(variable) %>%
  t() %>%
  paste(collapse = " + ") %>%
  paste("corg ~", .) %>%
  as.formula()
training_index <- lapply(
  unique(data_all$site),
  function(s) which(data_all$site != s)
)
rf_vif <- train(
  form = formula_soc_vif,
  data = data_all,
  method = "rf",
  metric = "RMSE",
  trControl = trainControl(method = "cv", index = training_index),
  ntree = 500, importance = TRUE
)
save(rf_vif, file = "data/cache/models/rf_vif.rda")


Model performance was evaluated with a leave-one-site-out cross-validation, which yielded the following metrics:


RMSE: 0.4806444 
R²: 0.159598

title: Variable selection
section: RandomForest with variable selection based on VIF + FFS
text:
RandomForest with variable selection based on VIF + FFS
This method was inspired by the work from Traoré et al. (2024).

Environmental variables were grouped as: climate - temperature / climate - precipitation / soil - physical variables / soil - chemical variables / topography / landuse.
For each group, a first variable selection was done to avoid collinearity, by applying a using a Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) threshold of < 5 with a stepwise procedure (Naimi et al. 2014).
A second variable selection step is then performed for each group of variables using a forward feature selection (FFS) procedure (Meyer et al. 2025).
All selected variables are then grouped, and the VIF + FFS are applied to this subset of variables.
The RandomForest model is then calibrated with the final subset of variables.



Code
# create groups
data_groups <- data.frame(
  variable = setdiff(
    colnames(data_all),
    c("site", "plot", "lon", "lat", "T_stock", "corg")
  )
) |>
  mutate(group = ifelse(grepl("bio|LST", variable), "clim", "topo")) |>
  mutate(group = ifelse(grepl("_mean|NDVI", variable), "spectral", group)) |>
  mutate(group = ifelse(grepl("_age|landuse", variable), "landuse", group)) |>
  mutate(group = ifelse(grepl("WRB2", variable), "soil", group)) |>
  mutate(group = ifelse(grepl("MEM", variable), "spatial", group)) |>
  group_by(group) |>
  # vif selection by group, based on variables selected at previous stage
  mutate(vif1 = vif_select(variable, data_all)) |>
  # ffs by group, based on variables selected at previous stage
  mutate(ffs1 = ffs_select(variable, data_all, vif1, response = "corg")) |>
  ungroup() |>
  # vif selection for all, based on variables selected at previous stage
  mutate(vif2 = vif_select(variable, data_all, ffs1)) |>
  # ffs for all, based on variables selected at previous stage
  mutate(ffs2 = ffs_select(variable, data_all, vif2, response = "corg"))
write.csv(data_groups, file = "data/cache/var_selection.csv", row.names = FALSE)


The following variables were selected for each group at the different steps:


Code
read.csv("data/cache/var_selection.csv") |>
  pivot_longer(cols = paste0(rep(c("vif", "ffs")), rep(1:2, each = 2))) |>
  group_by(group, name) |>
  summarise(vars = paste(variable[value], collapse = ", ")) |>
  pivot_wider(values_from = "vars") |>
  relocate(group, vif1, ffs1, vif2, ffs2) |>
  kable(col.names = c(
    "Groups",
    paste(rep(c("VIF", "FFS")),
      rep(c("group", "all"), each = 2),
      sep = "_"
    )
  ))

pMEM_18, pMEM_10, pMEM_29, pMEM_119, pMEM_528, pMEM_219
pMEM_18, pMEM_10, pMEM_119, pMEM_528, pMEM_219

Code
formula_soc_ffs <- read.csv("data/cache/var_selection.csv") %>%
  filter(ffs2) %>%
  select(variable) %>%
  t() %>%
  paste(collapse = " + ") %>%
  paste("corg ~", .) %>%
  as.formula()
training_index <- lapply(
  unique(data_all$site),
  function(s) which(data_all$site != s)
)
rf_ffs <- train(
  form = formula_soc_ffs,
  data = data_all,
  method = "rf",
  metric = "RMSE",
  trControl = trainControl(method = "cv", index = training_index),
  ntree = 500, importance = TRUE
)
save(rf_ffs, file = "data/cache/models/rf_ffs.rda")


Model performance was evaluated with a leave-one-site-out cross-validation, which yielded the following metrics:

title: Variable selection
section: Comparison of Random forest models performance
text:
Comparison of Random forest models performance


Code
rf_models <- lapply(c("all", "vif", "ffs"), function(model) {
  load(paste0("data/cache/models/rf_", model, ".rda"))
  get(paste0("rf_", model))
})
names(rf_models) <- c("All Variables", "VIF selection", "VIF+FFS selection")
lapply(rf_models, function(md) md$results) %>%
  data.table::rbindlist(idcol = "model") %>%
  select(model, mtry, RMSE, Rsquared, MAE) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(RMSE, Rsquared, MAE),
    names_to = "metric", values_to = "value"
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = mtry, y = value, color = model)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  facet_wrap(~metric, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    title = "Comparison of performance by mtry",
    x = "mtry (number of variables sampled)",
    y = "Metric value",
    color = "Model"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()













Meyer, Hanna, Carles Milà, Marvin Ludwig, Jan Linnenbrink, and Fabian Schumacher. 2025. CAST: ’Caret’ Applications for Spatial-Temporal Models. https://github.com/HannaMeyer/CAST.


Naimi, Babak, Nicholas a.s. Hamm, Thomas A. Groen, Andrew K. Skidmore, and Albertus G. Toxopeus. 2014. “Where Is Positional Uncertainty a Problem for Species Distribution Modelling.” Ecography 37: 191–203. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1600-0587.2013.00205.x.


Traoré, Soulemane, Irie Casimir Zo-Bi, Camille Piponiot, Raphaël Aussenac, and Bruno Hérault. 2024. “Fragmentation is the main driver of residual forest aboveground biomass in West African low forest-high deforestation landscapes.” Trees, Forests and People 15 (March): 100477. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tfp.2023.100477.

title: Spatial structure
text:
Non-spatial random forest
As the samples were grouped by sampling site, our dataset is spatially aggregated. Traditional random forest models do not account for the spatial structure of such data, particularly the presence of spatial autocorrelation. To address this issue, we evaluated the impact of incorporating spatial autocorrelation by using the Random Forest Spatial Interpolation (RFSI) approach (Sekulić et al. 2020). This approach was found to offer slightly improved performance while remaining computationally efficient in a separate national-scale study of soil organic carbon stock mapping (Kmoch et al. 2025).
The model was fitted with the response variable (soil organic carbon stock) and the complete set of predictor variables. Model accuracy metrics from the cross validation steps were:
Code
load("data/cache/models/rf_all.rda")
cat(
  "RMSE:", mean(rf_all$resample$RMSE),
  "\nR²:", mean(rf_all$resample$Rsquared)
)


RMSE: 0.4603164 
R²: 0.1629922

title: Spatial structure
text:
Random Forest with Spatial Interpolation (RFSI)
To account for spatial autocorrelation, a Random Forest Spatial Interpolation (RFSI) model was implemented using the rfsi package. The RFSI model incorporates spatial neighborhood information into the Random Forest framework, allowing predictions that combine covariates with spatial proximity. Model accuracy metrics from the cross validation steps were:


Code
data_spatial <- st_as_sf(data_all, coords = c("lon", "lat"), crs = 4326)
rfsi_model <- rfsi_llo(data_spatial, formula_soc, data_all$site)
rfsi_results <- rfsi_model$cv_results
save(rfsi_results, file = "data/cache/models/rfsi_results.rda")




Code
load("data/cache/models/rfsi_results.rda")
cat(
  "RMSE:", rfsi_results[which.min(rfsi_results$RMSE), 2],
  "\nR²:", rfsi_results[which.min(rfsi_results$RMSE), 3]
)


RMSE: 0.53206 
R²: 0.14535

title: Spatial structure
Model results
text:
Based on the tests in the “Methodological choices” section, we used the model with variables selected with the VIF+FFS method and no spatial interpolation.


Code
data_all <- read.csv("data/cache/data_stocks.csv") %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/climate_data.csv")) %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/spectral_data.csv")) %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/topo_data.csv")) %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/thermal_data.csv")) %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/data_landuse.csv")) %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/tmf_data.csv")) %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/soil_data.csv")) %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/pmem_data.csv")) %>%
  drop_na()
sel_vars <- read.csv("data/cache/var_selection.csv") %>%
  filter(ffs2) %>%
  select(variable) %>%
  unlist()
qrf_final <- qrf_llo(
  data = data_all,
  ycol = "corg",
  xcols = sel_vars,
  site = data_all$site,
  nodesizes = c(5, 10, 15),
  ntree = 200
)
save(qrf_final, file = "data/cache/models/qrf_final.rda")

title: Effect of explanatory variables
text:
The importance of each selected variable in the final model is represented below.


Code
load("data/cache/models/qrf_final.rda")
var_names <- data.frame(
  variable = paste0("bio", paste0(c(rep("0", 9), rep("", 10)), 1:19)),
  var_name = c(
    "Annual Mean Temp", "Mean Diurnal Range", "Isothermality",
    "Temp Seasonality", "Max Temp of Warmest Month",
    "Min Temp of Coldest Month", "Temp Annual Range",
    "Mean Temp of Wettest Quarter", "Mean Temp of Driest Quarter",
    "Mean Temp of Warmest Quarter",
    "Mean Temp of Coldest Quarter",
    "Annual Precipitation", "Precipitation of Wettest Month",
    "Precipitation of Driest Month", "Precipitation Seasonality",
    "Precipitation of Wettest Quarter", "Precipitation of Driest Quarter",
    "Precipitation of Warmest Quarter", "Precipitation of Coldest Quarter"
  )
) |>
  bind_rows(data.frame(
    variable = c(
      "twi", "def_age", "Annual_Mean_LST",
      "present_landuse", "WRB2", "SWIR1_mean", "pMEM_10"
    ),
    var_name = c(
      "Topographic Wetness Index", "Time since deforestation",
      "Land Surface Temp", "Present Landuse", "Soil type",
      "Short-wave infrared 1", "Spatial structure"
    )
  ))
importance_qrf_llo(qrf_final, type = 1) |>
  left_join(var_names) |>
  left_join(read.csv("data/cache/var_selection.csv")) |>
  mutate(variable = ifelse(is.na(var_name), variable, var_name)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(variable, importance), y = importance, fill = group)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  coord_flip() +
  xlab("Variables") +
  ylab("Increase in Node Purity") +
  theme_minimal()









The partial dependence plots are represented below.


Code
data_all <- read.csv("data/cache/data_stocks.csv") %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/climate_data.csv")) %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/spectral_data.csv")) %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/topo_data.csv")) %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/thermal_data.csv")) %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/data_landuse.csv")) %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/tmf_data.csv")) %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/soil_data.csv")) %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/pmem_data.csv")) %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  setDT()

preds <- lapply(qrf_final$xcol, function(x) {
  if (is.numeric(data_all[[x]])) {
    data.frame(
      value = seq(
        min(data_all[[x]]),
        max(data_all[[x]]),
        length.out = 50
      )
    )
  } else {
    data.frame(value = unique(data_all[[x]]))
  }
})
names(preds) <- qrf_final$xcol
preds <- rbindlist(preds, idcol = "var")

preds[, c("Q10", "Q50", "Q90") := qrf_partial(
  qrf_final, var, value,
  data_all, c(0.1, 0.5, 0.9)
), .(var, value)]
write.csv(preds, "data/cache/partial_predictions.csv", row.names = FALSE)




Code
idcols <- c("site", "plot", "lon", "lat", "T_stock", "corg")
data_quant <- read.csv("data/cache/data_stocks.csv") %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/climate_data.csv")) %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/spectral_data.csv")) %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/topo_data.csv")) %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/thermal_data.csv")) %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/data_landuse.csv")) %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/tmf_data.csv")) %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/soil_data.csv")) %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  select(where(is.numeric) | all_of(idcols)) %>%
  pivot_longer(!idcols, names_to = "var") %>%
  filter(var %in% qrf_final$xcols) |>
  mutate(variable = var) |>
  left_join(var_names) |>
  mutate(variable = ifelse(is.na(var_name), variable, var_name))
data_qual <- read.csv("data/cache/data_stocks.csv") %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/data_landuse.csv")) %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/tmf_data.csv")) %>%
  inner_join(read.csv("data/cache/soil_data.csv")) %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  select(where(is.character) | all_of(idcols)) %>%
  pivot_longer(!idcols, names_to = "var") %>%
  filter(var %in% qrf_final$xcols) |>
  mutate(variable = var) |>
  left_join(var_names) |>
  mutate(variable = ifelse(is.na(var_name), variable, var_name))

gquant <- read.csv("data/cache/partial_predictions.csv") |>
  filter(var %in% unique(data_quant$var) & !grepl("MEM", var)) |>
  rename(variable = var) |>
  left_join(var_names) |>
  mutate(variable = ifelse(is.na(var_name), variable, var_name)) |>
  mutate(value = as.numeric(value)) |>
  ggplot(aes(value, Q50)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = Q10, ymax = Q90), alpha = 0.1) +
  geom_point(data = data_quant, aes(y = corg, col = site), alpha = 0.2) +
  geom_line() +
  facet_wrap(~variable, scales = "free", ncol = 3) +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  labs(
    x = "Variable value",
    y = "Distribution of predicted and observed SOC (%)"
  )
gqual <- read.csv("data/cache/partial_predictions.csv") |>
  filter(var %in% unique(data_qual$var)) |>
  rename(variable = var) |>
  left_join(var_names) |>
  mutate(variable = ifelse(is.na(var_name), variable, var_name)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = value, y = Q50)) +
  geom_point(data = data_qual, aes(y = corg, col = site), alpha = 0.2) +
  geom_pointrange(aes(ymin = Q10, ymax = Q90)) +
  geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(~variable, scales = "free") +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1)
  ) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL)
in_plot <- FALSE
if (in_plot) {
  ggdraw() +
    draw_plot(gquant) +
    draw_plot(gqual, x = 0.35, y = 0.01, width = .6, height = .33)
}
ggpubr::ggarrange(gquant, gqual, ncol = 1, heights = c(2, 1))





Partial dependence plots showing the effect of individual variables on predicted SOC stocks. Solid lines represent the median predictions, while the grey areas represent the 10–90% quantiles. The points represent observations, with each colour representing a different site.

title: SOC prediction
